attributes.stem:
A 72-year-old woman presents with fatigue for the past year. This is accompanied with difficulty sleeping due to night sweats and lower back pain. She also mentioned that she has been constipated for the past few months, only passing stools once every five days.

Physical examination reveals pallor of the conjunctivae. Her lumbar spine is tender upon palpation, with limited flexion and extension due to the pain.

Complete blood examination shows normochromic normocytic anaemia and a platelet count of 110x10^9/L. Biochemistry test demonstrates elevated calcium of 3.4 mmol/L with a low parathyroid hormone level. Urine dipstick is positive for protein.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most urgent investigation?
options: Serum protein electrophoresis for monoclonal paraprotein
attributes.explanation:
The most concerning diagnosis is multiple myeloma, with the major differential being a non-haematological metastatic cancer.

Symptoms of multiple myeloma can be remembered using the acronym CRAB - Calcium elevation, Renal dysfunction, Anaemia, and Bone disease. However, it is important to note that initial presentation of multiple myeloma can vary in different patients. The most common presenting symptom of multiple myeloma is anaemia, with patients presenting with fatigue and shortness of breath upon exertion. Back pain is also a common presentation, reported in more than 50% of patients. Lytic lesions may also be present on X-ray films. Renal dysfunction due to multiple myeloma leads to the kidney's inability to clear excess calcium, increasing the serum calcium levels subsequently.

In this case it is likely that her constipation is a complication of her elevated calcium. The proteinuria is likely related to her renal disease, as the urinary Bence-Jones proteins associated with multiple myeloma do not cause a positive urine dipstick.

Serum and urine protein electrophoresis is the initial investigation in patients with suspected multiple myeloma. The test is highly sensitive in detecting monoclonal proteins found in the disease. It is also non-invasive and safe for the patients. While we have strong clinical suspicion for multiple myeloma based on the CRAB features, SPEP provides crucial information about the type and quantity of paraprotein present, which is necessary for diagnosis confirmation, risk stratification, and treatment planning. SPEP is also much less invasive than bone marrow biopsy and is typically performed first in the diagnostic pathway.

Although patients with multiple myeloma often present with kidney impairment, kidney ultrasound is not a useful investigation for the disease as it will not affect the management plan. Kidney ultrasound is indicated in patients with suspected kidney cysts or malignant tumours, which is less likely in this case.

(99m)Tc Sestamibi scintigraphy is indicated in patients with suspected hyperparathyroidism which is not a likely diagnosis due to the patient's low parathyroid hormone level.

Bone marrow biopsy is useful to investigate haematological malignancy, however it is an invasive procedure and should not be done as an initial investigation. While bone marrow biopsy provides definitive diagnosis of multiple myeloma, it is typically performed after SPEP has confirmed the presence of a monoclonal paraprotein. This stepwise approach ensures that invasive procedures are only performed when necessary and after less invasive tests have provided supportive evidence.

Iron studies can be useful in determining the cause of her anaemia, however it is a less urgent investigation. The patient's symptoms point to malignancy which should be urgently investigated first.
learningPoints: Symptoms of multiple myeloma can be remembered using the acronym CRAB - Calcium elevation, Renal dysfunction, Anaemia, and Bone disease
Serum protein electrophoresis is the initial investigation for suspected multiple myeloma as it can detect monoclonal paraproteins with high sensitivity.
references: Firkin F. "Screening for multiple myeloma" Australian Prescriber 2009;32:92-4. Available at: https://www.nps.org.au/australian-prescriber/articles/screening-for-multiple-myeloma
Oyajobi, Babatunde O. "Multiple myeloma/hypercalcemia." Arthritis research & therapy 9.1 (2007): 1-6.
Eslick, Talaulikar D. "Multiple Myeloma:from diagnosis to treatment." Australian Family Physician (2013): 42:10. Available at: https://www.racgp.org.au/afp/2013/october/multiple-myeloma
Silbermann R, Roodman GD. Myeloma bone disease: pathophysiology and management. Journal of bone oncology. 2013 Jun 1;2(2):59-69.
